skill.name: angular-ui-patterns
skill.description: Modern Angular UI patterns for loading states, error handling, and data display. Use when building UI components, handling async data, or managing component states.
skill.summary: Essential Angular UI patterns for robust user interfaces with proper loading states, error handling, and progressive disclosure.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Documentation-only skill containing Angular UI pattern guidance. All 63 static analysis findings are false positives: URLs are documentation references (not network calls), backticks are markdown code fencing (not shell execution), and crypto detections are text pattern misidentifications. No executable code, network requests, or command execution present.

content.user_title: Build Robust Angular UIs with Proven Patterns
content.value_statement: Creating reliable Angular interfaces requires consistent handling of loading states, errors, and empty states. This skill provides battle-tested patterns that ensure users always understand what is happening in your application.
actual_capabilities: Implement loading states that show only when no data exists, preventing UI flash
Create error handling hierarchies from inline validation to full error screens
Build empty states with clear calls-to-action for collections and lists
Apply progressive disclosure patterns using Angular @defer for performance
Design button states with proper disabled states and loading indicators during async operations
Structure dialog and modal patterns with proper lifecycle management
limitations: Patterns are specific to Angular 17+ with modern control flow syntax (@if, @for, @defer)
Requires Angular Signals and standalone components for full implementation
Does not cover CSS styling frameworks - focuses on component logic patterns
Examples assume TypeScript and reactive programming familiarity

use_cases.title: Frontend Developer Building Data-Driven Dashboards
use_cases.description: Implement consistent loading, error, and empty states across dashboard widgets that fetch data from multiple APIs with varying latencies.

use_cases.title: Team Lead Establishing UI Standards
use_cases.description: Define organization-wide patterns for state management, error handling, and user feedback to ensure consistent user experience across teams.

use_cases.title: AI Assistant Generating Angular Components
use_cases.description: Guide Claude or Codex to generate Angular components that follow proven patterns for state handling, avoiding common pitfalls like silent errors or missing loading states.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Loading State Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Angular component that displays a list of items. Show a skeleton loader when loading with no data, an empty state when the list is empty, an error state with retry when loading fails, and the actual data when available. Use Angular Signals and modern control flow.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a basic data display component

prompt_templates.title: Form with Validation and Submission States
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an Angular form component with reactive forms validation. Show inline error messages for invalid fields, disable the submit button during form submission, display a loading indicator on the button, and show success or error toast notifications after the async operation completes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating user input forms with proper feedback

prompt_templates.title: Progressive Content Loading with @defer
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement an Angular page that loads critical content immediately and defers non-critical sections like comments, related items, or analytics using @defer. Include placeholder, loading, and error states for each deferred block with appropriate minimum loading times.
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing perceived performance for content-heavy pages

prompt_templates.title: Confirmation Dialog with Optimistic Updates
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a delete confirmation dialog pattern that shows a modal asking for confirmation, applies optimistic UI updates when confirmed, rolls back changes if the server request fails, and displays appropriate feedback to the user throughout the process.
prompt_templates.scenario: Handling destructive actions with safety and feedback

output_examples.input: A component that fetches and displays user posts
output: Component shows skeleton loader on initial load (no data exists yet)
When data arrives empty, displays empty state with 'Create Post' button
When API returns error, shows error banner with retry option while keeping cached data visible
On successful load, renders the full post list with proper accessibility attributes

output_examples.input: A save button in a settings form
output: Button is enabled only when form is valid
On click, button becomes disabled and shows spinner with 'Saving...' text
On success, shows green checkmark with 'Saved!' then reverts after 2 seconds
On error, shows red X with error message and re-enables button for retry

best_practices: Always show errors to users - never silently catch exceptions without feedback
Display loading indicators only when there is no existing data to prevent content flash
Disable action buttons during async operations to prevent duplicate submissions
anti_patterns: Showing spinner while cached data exists - causes unnecessary visual flash during refetch
Swallowing errors in catch blocks without informing the user of failures
Allowing multiple rapid clicks on submit buttons by not disabling during processing

faq.question: When should I use a skeleton loader versus a spinner?
faq.answer: Use skeleton loaders for initial page loads and list layouts where the content shape is known. Use spinners for modal actions, button submissions, and inline operations where the content shape is unknown or the area is small.

faq.question: How do I handle errors when I have partial data available?
faq.answer: Show the existing data and display an error banner at the top or bottom of the affected section. This graceful degradation approach keeps the UI functional while informing users about partial failures.

faq.question: What is the minimum loading time for spinners?
faq.answer: Use a minimum of 200ms for loading states to prevent flickering. If an operation completes faster than 200ms, still show the loading state for the full duration to avoid jarring visual transitions.

faq.question: How should I handle form validation errors?
faq.answer: Show validation errors inline next to the affected fields only after the user has touched the field. Use clear, actionable messages that explain what is wrong and how to fix it.

faq.question: When should I use optimistic updates?
faq.answer: Use optimistic updates for non-critical actions where a rollback is acceptable, such as likes, follows, or non-destructive edits. Always implement rollback logic in case the server request fails.

faq.question: How do I make loading states accessible?
faq.answer: Use aria-live regions to announce loading state changes to screen readers. Ensure focus is managed appropriately after state changes, and provide text alternatives for visual loading indicators.
